title: Sentiment Analysis with LSTMs
concept: LSTMs excel at sentiment analysis because they track negation words (like 'not good') over long sentences, unlike Bag of Words.

exercises.prompt: Why is sentiment analysis tricky for basic computer programs that just count words?

options.text: Words like 'not' change the meaning of other words around them.

exercises.explain: Negations like 'not' change the meaning of the words that follow them, which requires memory to track.

cards.title: The Twist in the Words
cards.body: If you tell a basic AI 'I am not happy,' it might only focus on the word 'happy' and think you are thrilled! How do we teach AI to understand the full context of our feelings? We use LSTMs!

cards.title: What is an LSTM?
cards.body: An LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is a smart neural network that reads text word-by-word and remembers previous words. In sentiment analysis, it uses this memory to understand how words interact, like how 'not' flips the meaning of 'happy' to mean sad.

cards.title: The Detective Reader
cards.body: Imagine reading a mystery book. A simple word-counting app might see the word 'guilty' 10 times and assume the character is the villain. But an LSTM acts like a human reader: it remembers that the detective said, 'He is *not* guilty,' keeping track of that crucial 'not' all the way to the end of the sentence!

cards.title: Movie Review Showdown
cards.body:
Consider this movie review: 'I thought this film would be bad, but it was actually not that terrible.' 

• A Bag of Words model gets confused by 'bad' and 'terrible'.
• An LSTM remembers the word 'not' right before 'terrible' and correctly rates the review as positive!

cards.title: Myth: AI Needs Grammar Lessons
cards.body: Many people think that AI needs to know every single grammar rule to understand sentiment. In reality, LSTMs learn to detect patterns and context—like negation—automatically just by looking at thousands of example sentences!

cards.title: Tracking Context
cards.body: Let's see how different AI approaches read the phrase: 'Not bad!'

right.text: Remembers 'Not' modifies 'Bad' -> Concludes the review is actually good!

left.text: Counts 'Not' (negative) and 'Bad' (negative) -> Concludes the review is very bad!

exercises.prompt: Why might a simple Bag of Words model fail on the sentence: 'The concert was not thrilled'?

options.text: It does not know what a concert is.
It just counts the positive word 'thrilled' and misses the 'not' modifier.
It cannot read words longer than five letters.

exercises.explain: Bag of Words only counts words individually, so it misses the connection between 'not' and 'thrilled', incorrectly guessing it is positive.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LSTMs process sentences by looking at all words independently without remembering what came before.
exercises.explain: LSTMs keep a memory state as they process words one by one, allowing them to remember context from the start of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about how LSTMs read sentences.
exercises.sentence: Unlike simple word counters, LSTMs care about the exact ____ of the words in a sentence.
exercises.explain: Because word order matters (like 'not good' vs 'good, not'), LSTMs track the sequence of words rather than just counting them in a random pile.

exercises.prompt: Which of these reviews requires an LSTM's memory to correctly understand that the customer is happy?
exercises.explain: The second review uses 'never' and 'boring' together, which cancels out to mean it was exciting. An LSTM can track this double negation, while a simple model might just see the word 'boring' and assume it's negative.

exercises.prompt: How does an LSTM actually manage to understand that 'not great' means something bad, even if the word 'great' is at the very end of a long sentence?

options.text: It simply counts the number of positive words and ignores negative ones.
It randomizes the order of the words to find hidden meanings.
It passes memory state along the sequence to connect negation words with their targets.
It translates the sentence into another language first.

exercises.explain: LSTMs are specifically designed with internal memory cells and gates that allow them to carry the context of early words (like 'not') over many steps, making them far better at capturing the true sentiment of long sentences than word counters.
